title: Effective-resistance-reducing flows, spectrally thin trees and asymmetric TSP
speaker: Shayan Oveis Gharan
abstract: Given a $k$-edge-connected graph $G = (V,E)$, a spanning tree $T$ is $\alpha$-thin w.r.t. $G$, if for any $S \subset V$, $|T(S,V - S)| \leq \alpha.|E(S,V - S)|$. The thin tree conjecture asserts that for a sufficiently large $k$ (independent of size of $G$) every $k$-edge-connected graph has a $1/2$-thin tree. This conjecture is intimately related to designing approximation algorithms for Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP). We show that any $k$-edge-connected graph has a $\mathrm{polyloglog}(n)/k$-thin spanning tree.

proof_stages.label: ATSP benchmark and announced gap bound
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines asymmetric TSP, the Held-Karp LP relaxation and its integrality gap, recalls prior logarithmic-type bounds, and announces a polyloglog(n) upper bound on the gap, presently nonalgorithmic.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to combinatorially thin trees
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reformulates the ATSP task through k-edge-connected unweighted graphs and alpha-thin spanning/connected subgraphs, explaining that sufficiently thin trees in log n-connected graphs imply corresponding ATSP integrality-gap and approximation bounds, with randomized rounding giving the previous log n/k-type barrier.

proof_stages.label: Spectral thinness via graph Laplacians
proof_stages.summary: Graph Laplacians and their quadratic forms are introduced in order to define alpha-spectrally-thin trees, a stronger condition than cut thinness that is polynomially testable through eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Effective-resistance obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Effective resistance is identified as the spectral thinness of a single edge, implying every tree’s spectral thinness is at least the maximum effective resistance of its edges, and a k-edge-connected long-path construction shows that direct spectral thinness can fail badly.

proof_stages.label: MSS sufficient criterion for spectral thinning
proof_stages.summary: The Marcus-Spielman-Srivastava/Kadison-Singer machinery is used as a sufficient condition: if edge effective resistances, or their averages over every cut, are small, then a spectrally thin tree exists; this separates the desired L1 cut-connectivity condition from the stronger L2 effective-resistance condition.

proof_stages.label: Cut-preserving L2 symmetrization by an augmentation
proof_stages.summary: To bridge k-connectivity and effective resistance, the proposed strategy is to add a graph or positive semidefinite matrix D that is dominated by G on all cuts while improving the L2/effective-resistance structure, so a spectral tree in G+D becomes a combinatorially thin tree for G.

proof_stages.label: Good-edge set and generalized MSS step
proof_stages.summary: Since some graphs admit no cut-dominated D that reduces all effective resistances, the proof must also delete bad edges by finding a k-connected good set F and a matrix D with small effective resistance on F, then extend the MSS argument to obtain a spectrally thin tree supported on G despite F being sparse in some cuts.

proof_stages.label: Convex-program construction and consequences
proof_stages.summary: A convex program, motivated by a singleton-cut version and analyzed through its dual l1-metric structure, is sketched as the method for constructing the needed D and F, leading to polyloglog(n)/k thin trees and hence the polyloglog(n) ATSP integrality-gap theorem, with algorithmic MSS implementation and c/k thin trees left as major goals.
